Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but it rarely happened that way. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, which probably wasn't the most focused approach. With my extracurriculars and everything else going on, I'd end up cramming when I remembered something was due rather than following any real schedule.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch the course materials, then suddenly realize a deadline was approaching and panic-study. I tried to set regular times at the beginning of the semester, but that fell apart pretty quickly. The anxiety of falling behind made it even harder to stick to any routine because I'd avoid thinking about the course altogether.
Q3: Usually fear, to be honest. I'd log in when I suddenly remembered there might be something due or when I got an email notification that scared me. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything critical, which happened more often than I'd like to admit. The gamified tasks they introduced were somewhat motivating at first, but even those couldn't keep me logging in consistently.
Q4: Not really, no. There were brief periods where I tried to make it a habit, maybe checking in every couple of days, but it never stuck. The LMS felt more like a source of stress than a helpful tool, so I'd avoid it. When I did log in, seeing all the things I'd fallen behind on just made me want to close it immediately.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I could listen to them and absorb information better than reading. I watched quite a bit of video content because that's how I learn best. The discussion posts were also useful when other students explained concepts in different ways. Sometimes hearing someone else's perspective clicked better than the original lecture material.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and written assignments until the last possible moment, and even then I barely completed them. My assignment completion rate was terrible because I kept putting things off. The readings felt overwhelming, and since I'm more of an auditory learner, I'd tell myself I'd get to them later but rarely did. I also avoided anything that required sustained focus because my anxiety made concentration really difficult.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate relevance or when it matched how I learn best. The gamified tasks were interesting because they broke things into smaller chunks and felt less intimidating. If something felt like it would take too long or was too abstract, I'd convince myself it wasn't essential and skip it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was already drowning in required work I hadn't done. Required activities carried this weight of obligation that made them stressful, but optional ones I could guilt-free ignore. Ironically, some optional activities might have helped me more, but I never got to them because I was always playing catch-up on the mandatory stuff.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I downloaded lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone while commuting or doing other activities. I also used some external YouTube videos that explained concepts differently. My notes from when I actually attended classes were helpful too. A lot of my studying happened away from the computer entirely.
Q10: There were many times I'd be listening to downloaded lectures while walking or exercising, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also discuss course concepts with friends from my extracurricular activities who were in similar programs. Sometimes I'd spend hours thinking through problems in my head without ever logging into anything. The LMS only saw a fraction of my actual engagement with the material.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd download the lecture videos once and then watch them multiple times offline. My internet access is good, but I preferred having things downloaded so I could study without distractions from notifications. I'd access a resource once, download what I needed, and then the LMS would never see me engaging with it again even though I was.
Q12: Around midterms, everything fell apart for me. My anxiety spiked, I had major commitments with my extracurriculars, and I just couldn't face the course anymore. The more I fell behind, the scarier it became to even look at what I'd missed. I'd lie awake at night feeling terrified about failing but somehow couldn't translate that fear into action during the day.
Q13: Massively. When I was stressed or tired, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be because it just reminded me of everything I was failing at. My extracurricular commitments took up a lot of mental energy, and by the time I thought about coursework, I was exhausted. The confusion about what I'd missed made logging in feel overwhelming, so I'd avoid it, which only made things worse.
Q14: Many times. I'd log in, look at the materials, and just feel my mind go blank from anxiety. I'd watch videos but realize I hadn't absorbed anything because I was too worried about everything else I needed to do. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: All the time, and it's something I'm not proud of. I'd avoid assignments because starting them felt impossible when I was already behind. The fear of doing poorly made me not do them at all, which is completely irrational but that's how my brain worked. Even knowing something was important, the anxiety would paralyze me into inaction.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything, but by then it was usually too late to do it well. I'd suddenly become very active in the LMS right before something was due, frantically trying to catch up. The problem was that my deadline-driven panic wasn't enough time to actually complete things properly, which is why my assignment completion was so low.
Q17: When I did get feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly, but mostly it just confirmed my fears about how poorly I was doing. Negative feedback made me want to disengage more because it felt hopeless. The gamified elements gave some instant feedback that was less threatening, which helped a little, but it wasn't enough to overcome my overall avoidance pattern.
Q18: The course structure felt rigid in ways that didn't work for me. Missing one thing led to falling behind on everything else, and there wasn't much flexibility for catching up. The way content built on previous content meant that once I fell behind, everything became confusing. I needed more scaffolding and check-ins to stay on track.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it matched my learning style. But the heavy reliance on written assignments and readings made it harder for someone like me. The gamified tasks were a nice attempt to make things more engaging, but they couldn't overcome the fundamental mismatch between how the course was structured and how I actually learn best.
Q20: That's literally me. I'd log in, watch videos, participate in discussions, but then freeze when it came to actually submitting work. The engagement feels safer than the commitment of turning something in that will be judged. Also, sometimes you're trying to understand the material before you can do the assignments, so there's a lot of activity that doesn't translate into submissions.
Q21: I can see how that happens if someone studies efficiently offline or already knows the material. That wasn't my experience though—my low activity matched my poor performance. But I know people who barely logged in because they were studying from textbooks and external resources and did fine. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the discussions with peers outside the platform. They also can't capture the emotional state—whether you were genuinely engaged or just anxiously scrolling without absorbing anything.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious and want to understand, not just trying to survive until the deadline. For me, true engagement happened in rare moments when I wasn't overwhelmed by anxiety and could actually focus on learning something interesting.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how scared I was all semester, or how much I actually thought about the course even when I couldn't bring myself to log in. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while trying to calm my anxiety. It also wouldn't show that despite my terrible performance, I actually found some of the content genuinely interesting when I could get past my fear of failure.
